serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of pork intestines mala is approximately 200 g, served as a shared dish or individual main course.

nutrient_source.title: Pork, large intestine, cooked — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 10397)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base nutrient values derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked pork large intestine. Sodium and fat values adjusted upward to reflect mala spice paste, soy sauce, and cooking oil additions typical of restaurant preparation. Adjustment methodology: sodium increased by approximately 300 mg/100g to account for soy sauce and mala seasoning; fat increased by approximately 3 g/100g to account for cooking oil. These adjustments are conservative estimates consistent with comparable stir-fried offal preparations.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of protein- and fat-dominant foods: meat, offal, and organ meats — University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for pork intestines mala. GI estimated at 25 based on analogous high-protein, high-fat, low-carbohydrate cooked pork offal entries in the University of Sydney GI Database. Protein- and fat-dominant foods with minimal carbohydrate content consistently yield low GI values (typically 0–35). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (25 × 5.0) / 100 = 1.25, rounded to 1.3.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from mala spice paste and soy sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid pork intestines mala due to its high sodium content of approximately 620 mg per 100 g, which can contribute substantially to daily sodium limits when consumed in a standard 200 g portion.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index but high saturated fat and caloric density
type2_diabetes.guidance: Pork intestines mala has a low glycemic index and minimal carbohydrate content, posing limited acute glycemic risk, but its high saturated fat and caloric density warrant portion control for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes and associated cardiovascular risk.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High cholesterol, saturated fat, and sodium content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Pork intestines mala is high in dietary cholesterol (approximately 132 mg/100g), saturated fat, and sodium, all of which are established risk factors for cardiovascular disease, and regular consumption should be minimised in individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pork intestines mala (猪大肠麻辣) is a Chinese-origin dish consisting of cleaned and cooked pork large intestine seasoned with mala spice blend — a combination of Sichuan peppercorn, dried chilli, garlic, ginger, soy sauce, and sesame oil that produces the characteristic numbing (má) and spicy (là) flavour profile. The dish is popular in Singapore's Chinese food scene, particularly in hawker centres and casual dining restaurants, and is associated with Sichuan-influenced cuisine. The intestines are typically cleaned thoroughly, blanched, then braised or stir-fried with the mala paste until tender and deeply flavoured. The texture is soft and slightly chewy with a rich, intensely spiced coating. Nutritionally, pork intestines mala is a high-protein, high-fat, moderate-calorie dish with significant cholesterol and sodium content. It provides approximately 215 kcal, 14.8 g protein, and 16.2 g fat per 100 g. The high sodium content (approximately 620 mg/100g) and cholesterol load make it a food of concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is low due to minimal carbohydrate content, making acute glycemic impact limited, though the high saturated fat content remains relevant for Type 2 Diabetes management. The dish is not halal due to pork content and contains soy and sesame allergens.

confidence_reason: No exact match for pork intestines mala found in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as a prepared dish. Nutrient values adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked pork large intestine with conservative adjustments for mala seasoning and cooking oil. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is medium rather than low because the base ingredient data is from an approved primary source and adjustments are methodologically documented.
notes: 1. Cuisine tag in input JSON was listed as 'Italian' with tags for Pizza, Coffee, Toast — this is clearly a data entry error for a restaurant (The Light Cafe - High Street Plaza) that serves diverse items. The food itself is Chinese in origin and has been reclassified accordingly. 2. The Burmese script in the original food name (ဝက်အူမာလာ) has been preserved in the aliases list as a romanized/script alias. 3. Vitamin A (vitamin_a_mcg_rae) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for cooked pork large intestine. 4. Folate (folate_mcg_dfe) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this preparation. 5. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for cooked pork large intestine. 6. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this preparation. 7. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for cooked pork large intestine. 8. GI set to 25 as an analogous estimate for a high-protein, high-fat, low-carbohydrate cooked pork offal dish; gi_category is low (GI ≤ 55) per Veritas threshold rules. 9. All -1 sentinel values are documented above per g3 sentinel values documentation rule.
